title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 57: Qwen Image Generation Workflow for Stunning Results
description:
In this episode of our ComfyUI tutorial series, we take a deep look at the Qwen model, a surprisingly powerful AI image generator with exceptional prompt understanding and the rare ability to produce clear, accurate text in images. Whether you’re designing game interfaces, product concepts, or complex illustrations, Qwen delivers high-quality results with fewer mistakes.

You’ll learn:
- How to install and set up Qwen in ComfyUI (including required models and custom nodes)
- How to run txt2img and img2img workflows
- Tips for full HD and 2K generation without upscaling
- How Qwen compares to Flux and CreArt models for realism, design, and speed

We’ll also test Qwen with illustrations, advertising images, game UI design, realistic photography, steampunk art, and text-heavy compositions, and compare results so you can see where it shines and where it still has room to improve.

Bonus: We explore a fast Nunchaku workflow with another model worth trying if you want quick generation times

Make sure your Comfy UI is updated to use these workflows.  I like to go to the Update folder and run the Update Comfy UI Bat file,  then I just restart Comfy UI.  Sometimes certain nodes in Comfy UI need to be updated  so they can recognize new models.  If you get an error with some nodes, open the Manager, click Update All,  and then restart Comfy UI. You need four models to run this workflow and also four custom nodes.  In this workflow, I used the Quenn Q8 version,  but depending on your video card, you should try a model that fits your setup.  The smaller the Q version, the lower the quality, but it will run faster.  I tested Q8 and Q6 and had similar speed,  with Q8 being about 1 to 2 seconds faster on my RTX 4090 and the quality was very similar.  A friend of mine tested the Q4 model on 12GB of VRAM and it worked fine.  Try different versions to see which one works best for you.  You download each of the models from the Pixorama note,  then go to your Comfy UI folder, find the model's directory,  and place each model in the correct folder.  For the Quenn image model, place it in the diffusion model's folder.  You do the same for the Clip model and place it in the Clip folder,  then put the VAE model in the VAE folder,  and the four steps Laura in the Laura's folder.  The upscaler you should already have it,  but if not, you can download it as well.  From the manager and custom nodes manager install these nodes,  but if you followed the previous tutorials, you should already have them.  If you are missing a node, Comfy UI will notify you,  and you will see a red outline around the nodes.  If you downloaded a different Q version model,  make sure you select the downloaded version in this node,  and do the same for the rest of the nodes.  If you cannot see the model in the node, go to Edit and Refresh node definitions.  Let's run the workflow.  The first time it will be a little slower,  but the second time it should run faster.  And this is the result.  As you can see, it is pretty good with text.  In fact, I haven't seen another free model that is this good with text so far. It will upscale the same image because the seed is fixed,  so if you want to generate a new one,  you need to change the seed manually.
